key_points: Deforestation (disappearance of forests) became a major issue during the colonial period to meet the needs of the British Empire.
The British needed timber for two main reasons: Building ships for the Royal Navy and sleepers for the expanding Railway network.
In 1864, the British set up the Indian Forest Service and appointed Dietrich Brandis, a German expert, as the first Inspector General of Forests.
The Indian Forest Act of 1865 (amended in 1878) divided forests into three categories: Reserved, Protected, and Village forests.
Reserved Forests were the 'best' forests where villagers were not allowed to take anything, even for their own use.
Scientific Forestry was introduced, which involved cutting down natural diverse forests and replacing them with one type of tree planted in straight rows (plantations).
Traditional 'Shifting Cultivation' (Swidden agriculture) was banned by the British because they felt it made it difficult to calculate taxes and was a fire hazard.
The rebellion in Bastar (1910) and the Dutch struggle in Java (Samin's Challenge) were major movements against colonial forest laws.
key_terms: Sleepers: Wooden planks laid across railway tracks to hold the rails in position.
Scientific Forestry: A system of cutting trees and replanting them in a planned, industrial manner.
Reserved Forests: Forests where villagers had no access and all resources belonged to the state.
Shifting Cultivation: A practice where parts of the forest are burnt and cleared in rotation to grow crops.
Blandongdiensten: A system in Java where the Dutch exempted villagers from rent if they provided free labor for cutting timber.
important_figures: Dietrich Brandis: The German expert who helped the British set up the forest management system in India.
Gunda Dhur: A tribal leader from the village of Nethanar who led the Bastar rebellion against the British.
Surontiko Samin: A villager in Java who started a movement against the Dutch, questioning state ownership of the forest.
exam_tips: Remember why the British hated Shifting Cultivation: They couldn't grow 'valuable' timber like teak and sal on those lands.
Focus on the 'Railway' connection: Every mile of railway track required between 1,760 and 2,000 sleepers.
Differentiate between the three types of forests: Reserved (No entry), Protected (Some rights), and Village (Local use).
Notice the pattern: Both in India (Bastar) and Indonesia (Java), the colonial powers took away forest rights, leading to tribal revolts.
The year 1906 is important for the Imperial Forest Research Institute set up at Dehradun.
